title: Fidelity-Aware Frequency Allocation and Transpilation Co-Design for Tunable Coupler Quantum Systems
authors: Dylan VanAllen, Evan McKinney, Israa G. Yusuf, Girgis Falstin, Gaurav Agarwal, Jason Pollack, Michael Hatridge, Alex K. Jones
abstract: Frequency crowding is a fundamental limitation in superconducting quantum architectures, particularly in tunable-coupler systems. We present a framework that explicitly models both coherent spectator-induced errors and incoherent lifetime effects through an error budgeting approach. Using this model, we analyze how frequency crowding impacts gate fidelity as module size and connectivity scale, and formulate a constrained optimization problem to assign qubit and coupler frequencies under realistic separation and hardware constraints. We demonstrate scalable frequency allocation strategies that minimize spectator-induced errors. We further show that increasing qubit count and coupling density within a module leads to a fidelity-connectivity tradeoff. To explore the benefits at the system scale, we have developed a noise-aware transpilation approach called FINESSE, which minimizes error by selecting high-fidelity paths that satisfy connectivity via SWAP insertion while jointly optimizing downstream gate execution. We demonstrate this physics-informed architecture-transpilation co-design approach for a SNAIL-based third-order coupler that natively realizes the $\sqrt{iSWAP}$ basis with frequency aware gate fidelities. On SNAIL architectures, FINESSE achieves an average 8.9% reduction in log-infidelity cost and 6.8% reduction in circuit depth vs. SABRE. We also compare results on IBM Brisbane's architecture.
summary: The paper proposes a co-design strategy that simultaneously optimizes hardware frequency allocation and software circuit transpilation to mitigate errors in tunable-coupler quantum computers. By accounting for hardware-specific noise during the routing process, the proposed FINESSE algorithm significantly improves gate fidelity compared to standard compilers. This approach effectively manages the tension between high connectivity and the detrimental effects of frequency crowding.
main_problem: The paper addresses the fundamental tradeoff between connectivity and gate fidelity in superconducting quantum architectures, specifically how frequency crowding in tunable-coupler systems leads to parasitic interactions and degraded performance.
main_result: The authors developed FINESSE, a noise-aware transpilation framework that achieves up to an 8.9% reduction in log-infidelity on SNAIL architectures and a 12.3% reduction on IBM Brisbane, demonstrating that joint optimization of frequency allocation and circuit mapping is superior to standard methods.
method: The study uses an error-budgeting framework to model coherent and incoherent errors, a constrained optimization approach for frequency assignment, and a novel transpilation algorithm (FINESSE) that uses fidelity-weighted distances for SWAP selection.
model_or_system: The research focuses on superconducting quantum architectures using SNAIL-based tunable couplers and transmon qubits, specifically modeling the 'Corral' module and evaluating performance on the 127-qubit IBM Brisbane heavy-hex processor.
key_observables: Gate fidelity, circuit depth, log-infidelity cost, and connectivity/edge count.
important_parameters: Pump strength (eta), qubit and SNAIL frequencies, detuning (delta), gate duration, and the fidelity-connectivity tradeoff limit (4 qubits per SNAIL).
main_assumptions: The model assumes a weak-coupling approximation where spectator-induced errors are additive and uses a conservative amplitude bound for spectator interaction to avoid reliance on precise Rabi synchronization.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the co-design workflow; Figure 2 details the SNAIL-qubit coupling and compatibility graphs; Figure 3 illustrates spectral positioning; Figure 13 compares log-infidelity across algorithms; Figure 15 shows fidelity degradation with increasing edge count; Figure 16 compares performance on IBM Brisbane.
paper_structure: The paper follows a co-design logic: first establishing the hardware-level problem of frequency crowding and error modeling, then presenting a frequency allocation optimization, followed by the development of the FINESSE transpilation framework, and concluding with performance benchmarks on both SNAIL and IBM architectures.
why_interesting: This is highly relevant for researchers in open quantum systems and quantum optics as it provides a rigorous framework for modeling how parasitic, non-local, and dissipative interactions (spectator errors and decoherence) impact the scalability of driven, multi-mode superconducting circuits.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. High-level workflow for the co-design of quantum computer architectures [1].

Figure 2. (a) SNAIL-qubit coupling graph (b) 2Q conversion connectivity graph, with unique colored edges for activated exchanges. (c) Compatibility graph, with nodes as interac- tions and edges as interferences.
Figure 3. Spectral positioning of a SNAIL and 4 qubit bare modes and their interacting resonant frequencies.

Figure 4. In this diagram, the central SNAIL is driven with the target interaction denoted by a green edge. Spectator terms diminish based on how many orders of hybridization they are removed from the driven SNAIL. (Blue) Both qubits coupled to the driven SNAIL (in the same module); (Orange) One qubit directly coupled to the driven SNAIL; (Purple) Neither qubit directly coupled to the driven SNAIL.
Figure 5. Infidelity versus detuning for spectator amplitude with Rabi oscillations compared with spectator amplitude using Rabi magnitude bound.
